I would like to remind you that we continue with the test of the new format for this debate, so there is no fixed speakers list. After the first round of speakers, on behalf of the groups, Members who receive speaking time or non-attached secretariat from the groups as well will be called to speak and will be reminded of their allotted speaking time. So keep an eye on the screens and then make your way to the lectern.
I'll first give the floor, on behalf of the Council, to Marie脗聽Bjerre, Minister for European Affairs, to start the debate.

They will not stop on their own. Our response must be clear, united and resolute. We must send a strong message to Russia: Europe stands together. We defend our security and we continue our support to Ukraine.
We need a truly pan-European mindset because the threats go far beyond the eastern border. In recent months, the EU has already taken strong action to improve its defence readiness. The SAFE instrument is one example. Last week, in Copenhagen, EU leaders were clear in their commitments to work together. They discussed progress on agreed priority areas that include, among others, air and missile defence, artillery, drones and anti-drone systems, military mobility and cyber-resilience.
As stated by President Costa, leaders in Copenhagen broadly supported priority flagships presented by the Commission and the High Representative. This includes the European drone initiative and the Eastern Flank Watch. The leaders provided guidance for the next step. The European Council in October will revert to the Defence Readiness Roadmap. Drones and counter-drone systems are a top priority.
Regarding the Eastern Flank Watch: there was also a meeting of defence ministers from the eastern flank countries on 26脗聽September that was hosted by Commissioner Kubilius and High Representative Kallas. Following the informal European Council last week, President Costa highlighted the need for increased coordination to keep track of progress and a greater role for defence ministers. The Eastern Flank Watch will be complementary to NATO's initiatives and actions at the eastern border, including the Eastern Sentry, the Baltic Air Policing and the Enhanced Forward Presence forces. EU-NATO cooperation remains essential to close capability gaps and to get European defence to the next level.
Drone incidents around Europe remind us that we are facing threats to our critical infrastructure. Such actions are testing our resilience and unity. EU and NATO must remain firmly and closely coordinated, both in our operational response and in our strategic communication. A coherent, clear, united stance is the answer to assuring our citizens that their security is being protected.
Russia is the most significant and direct threat to European and transatlantic security, not only because of its war of aggression against Ukraine, but also due to its systematic use of hybrid threats. Russia's hybrid activity activities continue to increase; our response needs to be adapted accordingly.
Already in October 2024, the EU condemned Russia's intensifying hybrid activity and adopted a new sanctions regime against Russia's actions. We will continue to make use of all available instruments to prevent and respond to Russia's hybrid activities. The EU is getting better prepared and getting more resilient, but we must not stop the course.

Make no mistake, this is part of a worrisome pattern of growing threats. Across our Union, undersea cables have been cut, airports and logistic hubs paralysed by cyberattacks, and elections targeted by malign influence campaigns.
These incidents are calculated to linger in the twilight of deniability. This is not random harassment. It is a coherent and escalating campaign to unsettle our citizens, test our resolve, divide our Union, and weaken our support for Ukraine. It is time to call it by its name: this is hybrid warfare, and we have to take it very seriously.
To those who still doubt, look at what is happening. One incident may be a mistake; two incidents, a coincidence. But three, five, ten 芒聙聯 this is a deliberate and targeted grey-zone campaign against Europe. And Europe must respond. We must investigate every incident, and we must not shy away from attributing responsibility, because every square centimetre of our territory must be protected and safe.
Honourable Members, Russia wants to sow division. We have to answer with unity. It was Italian pilots under NATO's Air Policing mission that escorted Russian jets from Estonian skies. Ukrainian experts are sharing frontline expertise to help Member States counter drone incursions. This is solidarity in action. But we must not only react; we must deter, because if we hesitate to act, the grey zone will only expand.
The founding mission of the European Union is to preserve peace. Today, that means having the capacity to deter aggression and provocation. Europe must urgently equip itself with the strategic capacity to respond: one that is ready to address the changing nature of warfare. We must develop and maintain it together, and we must be capable of responding in real time.
The work has already begun. We are seeing the biggest surge of defence spending in the history of the Union. The Readiness 2030 agenda will mobilise up to EUR脗聽800脗聽billion until 2030. Now we need a precise, pan-European plan, coordinated very closely with NATO, on how to move forward. This is the core of the scoping paper we presented to leaders in Copenhagen last week. In two weeks, we will present our Readiness 2030 roadmap that we call 'preserving peace'. This will not only set common objectives, but also very concrete milestones and timelines on the way to 2030, because we all know only what gets measured really gets done.
I want to focus on three points that are central to this work. The first point deals specifically with violations of our airspace. Here, I am talking about a new pan-European flagship: the Eastern Flank Watch and the 'drone wall'. The drone wall is our response to the realities of modern warfare. Just think about what happened in Poland. We had to deploy very expensive systems 芒聙聯 actually, latest-generation fighter jets 芒聙聯 to take down relatively cheap, mass-produced drones. This is absolutely not sustainable. We need an anti-drone system that is affordable and fit for purpose for swift detection, swift interception and, when needed, swift neutralisation. We have a lot to learn from Ukraine in this field. We have to learn a lot from Ukraine on capabilities but, even more importantly, I think, on their ecosystem of rapid innovation. Ukraine is ready to support our efforts.
The drone wall will contribute to the Eastern Flank Watch. This will monitor and protect the skies, seas and ground of our eastern Members. But, get me right, it is not only about our eastern border. At the moment it is our priority, but what we need is a 360脗潞 approach. This anti-drone system will be a shield for our entire Union, including of course, the southern flank. It should be designed to address a wide spectrum of challenges, from responding to natural disasters to tackling international organised crime, from monitoring weaponised migration to controlling the Russian shadow fleet. The list is long. Autonomous systems are at the centre of 21st-century security and Europe must be at the cutting edge.
Honourable Members, the protection of our people, our skies and our infrastructure requires more than drones. It requires drones, but more than that. This brings me to my second point: critical capabilities. We have already identified nine critical capabilities, from air defence to cyber to electronic warfare. For each of these, we will form collective capability coalitions that are groups of Member States committed to deliver together and fast.
We have seen this lead-nation model deliver before. Just think of the Czech initiative or the Danish-led initiative to provide weapons and ammunition to Ukraine. It was very successful. There also one nation took the lead. Others joined in to place larger orders; industry scaled up and prices went down. We will now expand this across the board. This is fast; this is efficient; and, most importantly of all, it is made in Europe.
My third point: none of this is possible without a strong European defence industry. We need to be able to rely on our own industrial base, specifically in critical times. Today, as we all know, most investment goes outside the European Union. In other words, these are jobs outside Europe. This is absolutely not sustainable.
Our surge in defence spending must also be an engine of growth here in the European Union. I know that partially this is already happening. We see new factories and production lines that are springing up across Europe. That's good. Our defence industry is hiring thousands of skilled workers. That is also very good. We see that the spill-overs are being felt in other industries too. We've seen, time and again, new technologies born in the defence sectors bringing new applications for civilian industries, whether it is from cybersecurity tools to, for example, advanced materials for shipbuilding and aerospace.
Our new defence investment can and must contribute to Europe's competitiveness. It must be based in our Union. That's why at least 65脗聽% of any project funded under SAFE must be from here, from the European Union. This is European money, and we want to see a return on investment of this European money in jobs here in Europe.
Honourable Members, tackling Russia's hybrid war is not only about traditional defence. It is about software for drones. It is about spare parts for pipelines. It is about rapid cyber-response teams. And it is about public information campaigns to spread awareness. This requires a completely new mindset for all of us. We must be ready to leave our comfort zone. We need to explore new ways of doing things. And, most importantly, we must deter everyone who seeks to harm our territory and our people.
The choice before us is very simple. We can either shy away and watch Russian threats escalate, or we meet them with unity, deterrence and resolve. I know which side we will choose. Thank you very much. Long live Europe.

Chaque jour, la Russie m脙篓ne contre nous des ing脙漏rences, des cyberattaques, des sabotages. Elle nous survole avec des drones et des avions de chasse. Ces menaces, nous les connaissons. Je ne vous apprends rien. Mais nos r脙漏actions ne sont toujours pas 脙聽 la hauteur.
Chers coll脙篓gues, nous devons assumer la confrontation avec la Russie. Nous devons monter d'un cran dans notre r脙漏ponse 芒聙聯脗聽je dirais m脙陋me, dans notre riposte. Car, chers coll脙篓gues, si nous voulons la paix, Poutine ne comprend qu'une chose: la force.脗聽Si nous voulons 脙陋tre libres, il faut 脙陋tre craints. Et pour 脙陋tre craints, il faut 脙陋tre puissants.
脙聤tre puissants, c'est augmenter les budgets consacr脙漏s 脙聽 la d脙漏fense de nos 脙聣tats membres. Ils se sont engag脙漏s 脙聽 le faire dans un temps record d'ici脗聽2030. Mais je le demande au Conseil: Madame la ministre, quand sortirons-nous pleinement de nos d脙漏pendances? Nous devons avoir la ma脙庐trise de la fabrication et de l'usage de nos armes pour enfin poser les fondations d'une souverainet脙漏 europ脙漏enne sur la d脙漏fense. 脙聤tre puissants, c'est accro脙庐tre nos investissements dans les infrastructures pour le d脙漏ploiement de mat脙漏riel militaire. 脙聤tre puissants, c'est 脙漏riger un bouclier a脙漏rien au dessus de l'Union europ脙漏enne et de l'Ukraine. C'est renforcer nos capacit脙漏s de d脙漏fense a脙漏rienne et la surveillance du flanc oriental. 脙聤tre puissants, c'est 脙陋tre capable d'abattre tout drone qui porterait un danger imm脙漏diat contre nous. C'est aussi sanctionner Viktor脗聽Orb脙隆n qui envoie des drones hongrois dans le ciel ukrainien. C'est scandaleux!

For that, we need European strength right now, because Putin will not succeed in weakening Europe by spreading fear. That's exactly the aim of all the things that are happening right now. And to you, Ursula von der Leyen, you are very happy to say that everyone who disagrees with you is a Putinist. But sometimes action speaks louder than words. So let's look at your historical actions: you were part of Angela Merkel's government. Not only part; you were Minister of Defence. You left Germany, NATO, defenceless in that period. More than that, you were an active part of moving away from nuclear power and into buying Russian gas and oil in the biggest energy consumer in Europe, Germany.
So to stand here and lecture those of us who believed in a strong defence, who tried to avoid us taking the peace dividend when you didn't see Russia as a threat is simply historical false narrative.
In the Danish People's Party, we fought against leaving submarines in Denmark. We fought against being defenceless when it came to our territorial defence. We fought in favour of the National Guard, in favour of conscription, when your allies in Denmark wanted to give it all up, and therefore Denmark is marginally better placed now to defend ourselves, even though we are falling hopelessly behind.
But please face up to your action, instead of blaming everyone else.

The recent incursion into European airspace showed that the threat is multifaceted, and that a wide range of means is used to destabilise us. The European Union must respond firmly to these hybrid threats. EU Member States must invest into anti-drone systems to protect their critical infrastructure against the drone attack. This is what Croatia has committed to, along with the participation in the drone coalition, to ensure a stable supply of drones to Ukraine to support production of drones in Europe. Moreover, to counter the Russian threat to our security, I welcome the European Commission proposal to develop European defence projects such as the Eastern Flank Watch. However, in designing this project, we must take into account the full scope of NATO's flank, from north to south, from east to west, Mr脗聽Commissioner, as we already discussed, and we also must increase cooperation with our American allies 芒聙聯 that remains key to the European continent's stability and security.

This should not come as a surprise to anyone 芒聙聯 this is straight out of Putin's playbook. For more than a decade, my home country, Sweden, has faced Russian airspace violations. In the Baltic Sea, Russia has targeted critical infrastructure. Putin's violations have become bolder, more reckless, more dangerous.
We have to unite to defend ourselves with real capability and force, strengthen our common defence, invest in tanks and ammunition.脗聽But it is also obvious, colleagues, that we have to do more to strengthen our capability with drones. This is, frankly speaking, more than a drone shield that we need to invest in.
I, therefore, would like to urge all Member States and the European Commission to seriously consider how to tackle this new Russian threat. This is about protecting our Union and our citizens, and making clear that Europe will never bow down to any aggression.

Unable to win on the battlefield, it now seeks to divide and intimidate Europe by financing corrupt politicians, spreading disinformation and striking our infrastructure to test our readiness.

Above all, we must discard peacetime thinking. This is how we will secure the victory.

Each drone incursion, each cyberattack, each interference attempt carries a clear message: to divide, to intimidate and to erode trust in the European project and our support to Ukraine.
Our response must be equally clear and united: Europe cannot afford hesitation or fragmentation. We must strengthen our collective defence, deepen intelligence cooperation and ensure that hybrid attacks are met with real consequences, not just statements.
At the same time, unity means more than deterrence; it means political, technological and moral solidarity. In fairness to the Ukrainians, they are doing a damn good job at it. A three day war, in Putin's eyes, has extended to three years and a lot longer. He has lost 1脗聽111脗聽000 troops and is not making great progress: a kilometre here or a kilometre there.

What we need to do is clear; let us remember an ancient Roman saying 芒聙聯 if you want peace, prepare for war, otherwise prepare for surrender if you want to have Putin-style peace. I would like to remind ourselves that it is Russia that waged a war against Ukraine, not Ukraine that attacked Russia. It is Russia that started provocations against Poland, the Baltic states, Copenhagen and other countries in Europe, not the EU that provoked Russia. In order to protect peace we need to ramp up our material defence readiness, first of all, which we are doing, but also our institutional defence readiness and our political defence readiness. Either we are politically and practically preparing to deter Putin or we are fighting each other. It is a choice for each of us.
Russia's provocations are reckless, irresponsible and dangerous, a threat to the people and peace of Europe. They must serve as a wake up call 芒聙聯 we need to act very practically and effectively. Drones are the future of war and the future of provocations. It is a pity that there was a need for the recent provocations to wake us up, and it appears that we are badly prepared for this future. At the moment, our defences are really not ready to detect drones and to see and to track them when they are crossing our borders. In addition, shooting down a EUR脗聽10脗聽000 drone with a EUR脗聽1脗聽million missile is really not sustainable. What we need to understand is that all of Europe is under threat 芒聙聯 and I mean all of Europe. A Russian Geran-2 drone has a 2脗聽500脗聽km range, and from Kaliningrad or Belarus it can hit targets in Denmark, Sweden, Belgium, Netherlands, Germany, northern France and Hungary, and it can even reach Brussels and Strasbourg. From container ships, drones can destroy targets all along the coast of Europe.
The question is: what needs to be done? There are a lot of things which we are doing and a lot of things which we need to do even more, but let me concentrate this time on drones and anti-drone capabilities. There is quite a broad agreement on what we need to do. In the short run, we need to develop capabilities to better detect, track and identify drones with acoustic sensors, radars, satellites. In the long run, capabilities to disarm or destroy drones, with drone interceptors, electronic warfare and mobile units. For the eastern flank countries there is also a need for capabilities to use the drones to hit the targets on the ground if an enemy tries to invade the country. A tailored approach, but that is what is needed. Frontline states have different needs compared to other countries, including countries in the Mediterranean, but all need capabilities to detect and to destroy drones.
Above all, what we need is cooperation, of course, to exchange data to track incursions across national borders. We have interoperable situational awareness to jointly identify targets and carry out operations. We will work hand in hand with NATO. We cannot do this without Ukraine; Ukraine's air defences take down 70芒聙聯80脗聽% of targets, Ukrainian drones chased the Russian fleet out of the Black Sea, brought the Russian advance to a standstill and devastated the Russian strategic bomber fleet and oil refineries. In the development of the drone wall special attention should be paid to Ukrainian experience 芒聙聯 this experience shows that the key to an effective drone wall is not just technology as such, but the developed skills of integrated command and the creation of a comprehensive new warfare ecosystem.
We must move quickly from ideas to actions. It is possible during the next year to build essential capabilities of the drone wall and by 2030 we shall have the possibility to spend EUR脗聽2.5脗聽trillion for different needs for our defence. I hope that frozen assets will be used to strengthen Ukrainian defence very soon; so there are financial means. I count on the support of this House with the ideas and to projects which we need to develop and are ready to work very intensively with the Parliament in order to rapidly develop needed capabilities. Thanks a lot.

Let us be clear: Russia will not stop. Therefore, we need strong unity and a strong response, and that is exactly why I get hopeful when listening to this debate today. It illustrates what is happening: Europe stands more together.
While Danes like Mr Vistisen from the Patriots criticise Denmark for receiving assistance last week during our informal European Council meeting in Copenhagen, it is not a sign of weakness. Denmark received police assistance from Sweden and Norway and military assistance from Germany and France. It is a display of unity. And what is about to happen? Europe stands more together. We need to show solidarity and action, as several of you said. That is what we do.
I agree with the comments from several of you 芒聙聯 not all, but the debate shows broad political support for a stronger Europe and a stronger response. Mr脗聽Halicki from EPP was advocating for a stronger defence industry in Europe. Ms脗聽Garc脙颅a P脙漏rez from S&D said that Ukraine needs to win. Ms脗聽Hayer from Renew said that we must be powerful if we want to be free. Ms脗聽Reintke from the Greens said that we need European strength.
All these comments, and many more, show that the EU is getting better prepared 芒聙聯 that we stand more united. But we must continue the course. We need to enhance our support for Ukraine to defend itself against Russia's war of aggression. This requires state-of-the-art technology, and Ukraine is a key partner in this regard. We need to learn from their battlefield experience and foster close collaboration on the defence industry with Ukraine, including on EU soil.
At the same time, we must continue to maximise pressure on Russia. This requires urgently strengthening the restrictive measures against Russia and stepping up our outreach efforts to other countries.
Last but not least, we need to ensure accountability for Russia's continued war of aggression in Ukraine. Russia's war crimes cannot be left unpunished.

Jens-Fredrik, welcome. Tikilluarit. And thank you for being with us. You stand here today on behalf of all the people of Greenland 芒聙聯 a people with a proud history and a strong future.
This Parliament knows it. And our message to you is this: when you look to Europe, you will always find an outstretched hand, a partner and a friend that stands with you in mutual respect. The ties between Greenland and the European Union run deep and they continue to grow. They have been shaped by the history that we share, by the challenges that we face together and by the way we see the world around us.
This House is grateful for the strong support for Greenland shown by Denmark in uneasy moments. It has led an EU drive of renewed partnership.
We meet at a time of uncertainty. The security and stability that we once took for granted can no longer be assumed. This was the clear message from European leaders in Copenhagen last week. And that is why, with Greenland as a strategic partner, particularly with Denmark holding the EU Presidency, we are working side by side to strengthen stability across the Arctic, to counter hybrid threats and to tackle growing security risks.
But our partnership goes far beyond security. It is about people, about making sure that communities, families and businesses in Greenland and in Europe can look to the future with confidence. That is why young people are at the heart of our partnership and that is why the European Union is investing in education and training in Greenland, with more than EUR 200 million already committed and more support on the way. This investment gives young Greenlanders the skills, the tools and the confidence to shape their own future and determine their own path.
So, dear Prime Minister, dear Jens-Frederik, thank you for being with us today. We look forward to hearing what you have to say, and to continuing our partnership in the years ahead. The floor is yours.

Your excellency, President Metsola, and honourable Members of the European Parliament, I am truly honoured for the invitation to address you, the parliamentarians of the European Union, here today. As you are the democratic voices of all the people in the European Union, I am eager to share with you my vision of Greenland's place in the evolving EU-Greenland partnership, a partnership that is of great importance to us.
2025 has been an eventful, if not dramatic, year for Greenland. The world is changing and it's changing fast. Greenland is no stranger to change. Our people, the Greenlandic people 芒聙聯 Kalaallit 芒聙聯 is a proud people who has survived and thrived in the harsh conditions of the Arctic for thousands of years. We have lived off the land and the ocean while respecting it and not taking more than was necessary. We have adapted our livelihoods to the climate and learned to recognise and react to the changes in our environment.
As we have dealt with these changes, one thing has remained constant: the recognition that we need each other. We need each other to deal with changes, both the challenges and opportunities. We would not have been able to survive if every member of our people did not contribute and fulfil a specific role. The country of Greenland is built on cooperation and the desire to cooperate based on fundamental values such as equality and respect for one another. This continues to be the core of Greenland today, also in our relations with the world outside of Greenland. We need cooperation and partnerships with like-minded countries and institutions who share our values.
The EU has been a stable, reliable and important partner for Greenland for more than 40 years. It has evolved from a cooperation primarily focused on fisheries to now including education, raw material, energy, biodiversity, research and climate change. Our relationship has gradually grown and expanded throughout the years, most recently with the EU office opening in Greenland, which has been a great tool for cooperation and connection.
The EU has been a steadfast friend and has stood by us when we needed it the most. I would like to extend a heartfelt thank you to you, the EU, for your strong support for Greenland during the challenging times we are going through right now.
You have stood by us as a friend and partner. We will never forget this. Thank you.
(Applause)
As we look ahead, we wish to continue to strengthen and develop our cooperation and partnership with the EU. As the world is changing fast, new challenges are presented. I believe that Greenland can offer solutions for the EU, and the EU can offer solutions for Greenland, creating new opportunities for us both on mutually beneficial terms.
The recent presentation of the European Commission's draft for the multiannual financial framework was followed closely by Greenland. We noted with great enthusiasm the presentation of the draft decision on overseas association, including Greenland. We were very happy to see the suggested focus areas for the cooperation with Greenland, namely education, critical minerals, renewable energy, digitalisation and sustainable tourism. These are all important areas where there is great potential to strengthen and develop our cooperation even further 芒聙聯 areas that can be beneficial for both Greenland and the EU.
We were also very encouraged to see that EUR脗聽530脗聽million is suggested to be allocated for cooperation with Greenland. We know that there will be long negotiations ahead where also you, as parliamentarians, will play key roles, but the suggested amount from the Commission is promising in terms of further developing our cooperation. This could, for example, include investments in the renewable energy sector and mineral resources sector, including infrastructure investment in different mining projects.
Our emerging mineral sector is poised to become a key player in securing supply lines of strategic and critical raw materials to the EU and to the world. Greenland holds 24 of the 34 critical minerals identified by the EU.
Developing mining projects is, however, capital-intensive. If we are to succeed in the exploitation of the critical minerals, there is a need to reduce the risk that investors take on. This task of reducing risks must partly be solved politically and not only by the markets.
In 2023, the EU and Greenland agreed on a strategic partnership on sustainable raw materials value chains. The objective of this partnership is to support the development of the mineral resources in Greenland, while also establishing raw materials value chains for the green transition in the EU. This partnership is a good and important step in our cooperation, and there are examples of projects where the EU has provided support. This is very positive.
Taking the current security situation in the world into account and the green transition, there is, however, a need to move faster. The critical minerals of Greenland have the potential to shift global and security balances, but action needs to be taken fast. Greenland is ready to move with the EU to hasten the pace.
It is not just our raw materials that are important for the security balances and the green transition; Greenland has the potential to become a key partner to the EU on renewable energy. Greenland's hydropower potential is both vast and untapped. With our abundant water resources, including glaciers and rivers, we are an ideal location for large-scale hydroelectric power projects. We have the potential to become a global leader in the export of green energy. This will create new income streams for Greenland and make a difference on a global scale. We are actively seeking investors for these projects, which are being offered in tenders next year. The EU Commission has already supported us in our first tender process and we are ready to collaborate further.
Greenland and our national utility company are currently in dialogue with the European Investment Bank to expand the hydropower plant near the capital, Nuuk. The engagement of the EIB in Greenland requires a framework agreement. Such an agreement is currently being negotiated between the relevant parties. Once the agreement is in place, we expect the EIB will be able to engage in other projects related to both infrastructure and raw materials. We look forward to exploring projects for such a collaboration further.
As we seek to strengthen our cooperation with the EU and other like-minded countries and institutions, connectivity becomes a key word. In today's world, no country and no economy can function and develop without strong connectivity.
In a country characterised by vast distances, dispersed settlements and harsh natural conditions, telecommunications are not merely a technical necessity 芒聙聯 they are a lifeline. The digital network connects our communities and supports education, healthcare and business, and it's essential for both the well-being of our citizens and the emergency response capabilities of our authorities.
Greenland is connected internationally through satellites and two submarine cables. Internally, we rely on a combination of land-based radio stations, submarine cables and satellites. Some places, especially in the far north of our country, are very poorly served with sustainable and very slow internet connections. We therefore have a strong need to enhance and strengthen our digital connectivity. We need to close the digital divide in Greenland.
Under the EU's Connecting Europe Facility, Greenland's national telecommunications company has been successful in getting grants to conduct seabed surveys in preparation for enhancing our submarine cable infrastructure. We are very keen to explore more such possibilities with the EU to enhance Greenland's digital connection to Europe. This would also include strengthening the satellite connectivity, particularly to our northernmost regions.
It is essential for us that those who operate telecommunications in Greenland are trusted and reliable actors. This is vital to ensure the stability of the infrastructure and to prevent unauthorised parties from disrupting and compromising connections.
(Applause)
As we seek to enhance our connectivity, we are also focusing on our flight routes. In 2024, we opened a new international airport in our capital, Nuuk. In 2026, we plan to open another international airport in Ilulissat, 350 miles north of Nuuk, as well as a regional airport in Qaqortoq in South Greenland. Our new airports are expected to increase our international connectivity, allowing for further integration into international trade and tourism. This will be positive for our cooperation with important partners like the EU.
Greenland and the EU have a long-standing cooperation on trade, especially when it comes to our fish and shellfish. The fisheries relations between Greenland and the EU date back to 1985, when Greenland left the European Economic Community.
Fisheries agreements have been in place since and allowed EU vessels to fish in Greenlandic waters in return for a financial contribution. It also provides duty-free access for Greenlandic fisheries products to the EU markets. This, however, hinges on a satisfactory fisheries agreement.
Today, our fisheries cooperation is regulated in the sustainable fisheries partnership agreement, which continues to be important for both the EU and Greenland. It, however, also remains vital to continue to adjust the cooperation on fisheries to develop and grow the Greenlandic fishing industry, which is the most important industrial sector in Greenland. We expect that the fishing industry will continue to remain the most important economic driver for Greenland in the years to come. For this reason, we must be agile and ensure that our relations continue to be beneficial for both of us.
Greenland is a country of proud fishermen and hunters. Not only do fisheries continue to be vital for our economy, but it's also crucial for the subsistence of our communities and families. The proud tradition of seal hunting also remains crucial for our communities. Seal hunting is an important source of income and sustenance for many families. For some of our communities, it is a vital part of people's livelihoods.
Seal hunting is also an important part of the Inuit culture, with knowledge and skills being passed down through generations. In our words, it is the livelihood of our culture and our identity.
The general ban on placing seal products on the EU markets has had a grave negative impact on seal hunting and has led to significant drops in the domestic supply as well as in the export of sealskin 芒聙聯 this all in spite of the exemption in the regulation for seal products derived from traditional harvests by Inuit and other indigenous peoples.
As the Commission takes the regulation up for evaluation in connection to the current fitness check, it is our sincere hope that the EU will consider and implement amendments that can alleviate the grave negative impact Greenland is experiencing from this ban. As we continue to strengthen and build on our relationship as friends and partners, we hope that we together can find a solution and a way forward for important issues such as this.
Our proud tradition of fisheries and hunting will continue to be a crucial part of the future of Greenland, with our youth standing ready to carry on the tradition. As we build and develop the future of Greenland, the education of our youth will be of utmost importance. The challenges and opportunities that are to come will require specific skills and knowledge to be able to handle.
Greenland and the EU have a strong and solid partnership in the field of education. It plays a significant economic role in the development of Greenland's education system and has opened doors for exchange of knowledge, resources and best practice. Through this cooperation, educational initiatives have been strengthened and the quality of teaching and learning in Greenland has been enhanced. We look forward to continuing to strengthen this important cooperation on education, which will ensure that Greenland remains a strong partner for the EU in the future.
As we look towards preparing a future for our youth and the following generations, one of the immediate challenges we are facing is the triple planetary crisis. This is a crisis that is putting Arctic ecosystems and populations under pressure. The Arctic is being transformed by accelerating climate change, transboundary pollution and biodiversity loss.
Through the cooperation between Greenland and the EU, important agreements and programmes addressing the triple planetary crises are being implemented in Greenland 芒聙聯 this on a local and national level, but also in an international context.
As an example, the establishment of a new national park in West Greenland can be mentioned, which is being financed through the cooperation with the EU. The traditional use of this area and its valuable biodiversity are now accompanied by increasing interest from tourism operators and other industries. Climate change is impacting the area, as well as the pollution and waste from the increased level of activity. A management approach for the national park that balances the biological diversity of the area, traditional use and sustainable development is becoming a reality thanks to the EU.
Moving forward, we hope to do more cooperation such as this in order to address the challenges of the triple planetary crisis.
The avenues for cooperation between us are many. Some of them we have just started to explore together and others have been developed over several years. Our partnership has gradually changed to fit the needs of Greenland and the EU. I believe that we now stand in a pivotal moment of time where our partnership once again needs to adjust to the unique challenges and opportunities we are faced with. If there is one message that I hope you take from what I have said today, it is this: Greenland needs the European Union and the European Union needs Greenland.
(Applause)
(The speaker spoke Greenlandic)
I will say it in English now.
(Laughter and applause)
On behalf of the Government of Greenland, I wish to express our appreciation for the continued development of cooperation between Greenland and the European Union, which we believe contributes positively to the advancement of our society.
Thank you once again for your loyal support and for a reliable and strong partnership, and finally, thank you for being a trusted friend of Greenland.
Qujanaq 芒聙聯 thank you.

We are about to vote on amending budget No脗聽2 for 2025 芒聙聯 a technical but essential update that keeps the European Union's budget balanced and credible.
Unlike in our national systems, where an amending budget often means fixing imbalances, in the EU it means ensuring that both revenues and expenditures remain aligned. This adjustment reflects the realities of our economy and the progress of our programmes.
On the revenue side, the figures are slightly lower than forecast, especially for customs duties, VAT-based resources and the plastic waste levy. But, of course, this is actually good news, because it means Europeans are recycling more, proving that our sustainability efforts are working.
On the expenditure side, the most important change is the additional EUR脗聽3.5脗聽billion for the rural development programmes. These are not just figures. They are direct investment in our farmers, in local communities and in the people who sustain Europe's food security. These funds, long overdue, are finally reaching the ground where they are needed the most.
We will also strengthen new areas of European governance, notably the establishment of the Anti-Money Laundering Authority, which will protect the integrity of our financial system and guarantee that EU money is clean, traceable and used for its true purpose.
To maintain a balanced budget, the GNI call rate will also be adjusted. It's a modest step, but one that keeps our finances sound and predictable.
At the same time, we urge the Commission to improve forecasting accuracy, accelerate the introduction of new own resources and ensure more flexibility in future budgets.
I therefore invite you to vote in favour of this report.

Are there any comments? No.

The sector also employs three million Europeans in direct and indirect manufacturing. But the automotive industry is also, of course, undergoing fundamental and profound change 芒聙聯 towards zero emission and towards digitalisation. On top of that, the industry is facing fierce 芒聙聯 and let me be very clear about it 芒聙聯 unfair competition from outside the EU. It is also facing lower sales, higher costs of energy and supply chain dependencies. That all affects its competitiveness, and that is true throughout the value chain.
If that were not enough, the world is also going through huge geopolitical change and turmoil. All of this requires our full attention and our action, and we need to make sure we find smart and workable solutions. That is why we are continuing to support the sector, so that the automotive companies can not only survive, but can actually thrive on European soil.
President von der Leyen and Commissioners Tzitzikostas, S脙漏journ脙漏, Roswall and myself have gathered automotive stakeholders this year for a series of strategic dialogues on the future of the European automotive industry. As a result, we now have the industrial action plan for the European automotive sector. What it does is it lays out what is needed to support the EU automotive industry's transition to clean, connected and increasingly automated vehicles and to make sure that they stay competitive globally.
Over the past months, several of these actions have been implemented and many others are coming up. Keep in mind, for example, the targeted amendment to the CO2 emissions standards for new cars and vans that we proposed in April of this year. It was adopted in record time, and it gives industry greater flexibility to comply with the target between 2025 to 2027, while leaving the overall targets unchanged. So, being pragmatic on the how and, we feel, it would also make sense to stay the course on the why and the what.
We do need to decarbonise the sector, as emissions from the overall transport sector are still above the levels of 30 years ago. We are doing it because it makes sense, not only for our climate goals, but also for our economic security and geopolitical reasons. Global players are speeding up investment, and more than one out of five cars sold in 2024 globally was electric. That is why we have set a clear framework for the transition to zero-emission vehicles with a clear target by 2035. This creates certainty for manufacturers and investors, while also providing enough time to plan a fair transition.
The objective is clear. Therefore, we should also aim to find a smart and workable way forward in the context of the review of the CO2 standard regulations. During the third strategic dialogue in September, we confirmed we will speed up work to come forward with the foreseen review by the end of this calendar year. We've launched a call for evidence and public consultation, which will close this week. We will carefully assess all inputs received as we are preparing the impact assessment.
In that context, the Commission will continue to commit to the principle of technological neutrality. The impact assessment will look into all main technology developments, with their potential, with their challenges 芒聙聯 be it plug-in hybrids, range extenders, e-fuels and other types of renewable fuels. As set out in the action plan, we also intend to come forward still this year with a proposal to decarbonise corporate fleets. Experiences from Member States have shown this to be a promising path to accelerate the transition to zero-emission vehicles.
The Commission will also soon come with a 'battery booster', with the aim to achieve near-term cost competitiveness of domestically produced cells and, of course, components. As announced by the President in her State of the Union address, we are preparing an initiative on small and affordable electric cars. Clean mobility, in our view, should be within reach for everyone.
Meanwhile, we will keep engaging with industry and with other stakeholders to monitor the situation and identify further strategic needs. In our view, with the right policy framework in place ensuring we deliver on the necessary enabling conditions and with such innovative and committed companies here in Europe, we are confident there is, as there should be, a thriving future for this hugely important sector.

Today, our automotive industry stands at the same crossroad, and history is clear: horse and carriage lost to the steam engine; the steam engine lost to the combustion car; and the combustion engine will be, for a big part, replaced by electric vehicles.
Instead of focusing on meeting of the current standard emissions, some start delaying with range extenders, very expensive synthetic fuels and other distractions. That's not innovation; that's sabotage. Because around the world, others are moving ahead: 42脗聽% of new cars in Vietnam are electric; Ethiopia has already stopped importing combustion engines; and Chinese EV sales grew 40脗聽% last year.
We owe Europe honesty and ambition. This debate today is not only misguided, it's also dishonest. We need to wake up and smell the coffee. Choosing for short-term profits will go at the expense of long-term gains for our auto industry, for cars being built in Europe.
Let's instead invest in electric corporate fleets, in social leasing programmes, so that the nurse, the teacher, the farmer can all drive electric cars, and not just the consultant, the lawyer and the banker.
Otherwise, I sincerely believe our car industry risks ending up like Kodak: as a mere snapshot of history.

Voglio fare per questo un appello agli amici del PPE. Su questi temi c'脙篓 una maggioranza possibile in quest'Aula. Lasciamo che si esprima con un voto, senza subire il perenne ricatto della sinistra, nemica dell'industria, del lavoro e dell'ambiente.

A blanket ban on internal combustion engines risks doing the opposite. It penalises technologies rather than emissions, discourages investment in new synthetic and renewable fuels, and weakens the global competitiveness of Europe's car industry 芒聙聯 an industry that employs millions and drives much of our research and exports.
True progress comes not from banning technologies, but from unleashing innovation across all technologies that reduce carbon. Electric mobility is essential, yes 芒聙聯 but so are low-carbon fuels, hydrogen hybrid solutions, new fuels and continuous efficiency improvements.
Climate policy should set targets, not dictate tools. A technologically neutral approach keeps Europe at the forefront of clean mobility. Let's lead with innovation, not prohibition.

What is at least very clear to me, with all the differences that I've heard, is that we all see that we are up for very significant change in this hugely important sector.

So, we know why we are doing it: again, for the economy, for climate and for independence. Our goals remain clear: first, to protect our citizens online, as we do offline. Second: to defend our autonomy in setting our own rules. The European Union is today a global leader in digital regulation. We have adopted groundbreaking legislation such as the Digital Services Act and the Digital Markets Act. Yet, with leadership comes responsibility not only to regulate, but also to ensure that regulation has a direct impact on people's lives.
The Digital Services Act aims to create a safe online environment for our citizens. Platforms have to remove illegal content and address the risk of disinformation and other harmful material. At the same time, the Digital Markets Act ensures that large platforms 芒聙聯 the so-called gatekeepers 芒聙聯 behave in a fair way online. Both of these acts set a high global benchmark. They establish clear obligations based on the importance of the online platform. In other words, bigger players have bigger responsibilities.
But these laws are now being contested. The US President has threatened tariffs against the EU. Large technology firms also resist. A recent example is Apple's call to scrap the Digital Markets Act. What I can say to these statements is that the rules apply regardless of the headquarters of the companies. They apply equally to companies of European, Chinese or US origin. To put it in short, the rules do not discriminate. They try to create a level playing field. Doing business in the EU is highly profitable for digital players. The single market is attractive for platforms, in particular for many US large online platforms and search engines. The cost of compliance with the DSA and the DMA is very low compared to the financial benefits of operating in the single market.
The EU has the sovereign right to regulate economic activities within its territory, in line with our own democratic values. We have always enforced and will continue to enforce our laws in a fair manner towards all companies operating in the EU. This is done in full compliance with global rules and in regular contact with online platforms. As in all other policy areas, we will continue to take the decisions we think are needed to ensure a fair and safe digital space. We count on the European Parliament as a counterpart in this effort.

European citizens and businesses have also signalled clearly that they want to see the DSA and DMA implemented effectively. Last month, President von der Leyen was crystal clear in her State of the Union speech here: when it comes to digital regulation, the EU sets its own standards and decides for itself.
So, clearly, this Parliament, the Commission, our citizens and European businesses agree: we need the DSA and the DMA. We need the DSA because it protects our fundamental rights online, because it protects our children from online harm, because it protects our democratic processes, and because it protects us from illegal, unsafe and unsustainable products. The DMA is important because it opens the door to the next waves of innovation, it brings the power of choice back to consumers.
The co-legislators gave the Commission a very strong mandate to enforce the DSA and DMA. Today, I can assure you that we are taking this duty very seriously.脗聽In April this year, we took the first two decisions under the DMA for Meta and Apple. We will not hesitate to take further action to ensure compliance. We also provided guidance to Apple to make interoperability a reality by the end of this year. Smartwatches, earbuds, speakers: no matter the brand everything will work seamlessly with your iPhones, just like Apple's own products.
Under the DSA, we have already designated 25脗聽platforms to whom we have addressed more than 150脗聽requests for information, and we have opened proceedings in 14脗聽cases, and have issued preliminary findings against providers of four very large online platforms. So workshops, roundtables, guidelines, work is ongoing all the time. We now have more than 250脗聽staff enforcing DSM and DMA, and we are hiring 60 more to further strengthen our hard-working teams.
We can already see many positive changes which have taken place thanks to these two laws. There is now much more transparency; thanks to the DSA, users can more easily exercise their rights. Many citizens have already used their right to complain when platforms are removing their content. As we can see on the DSA Transparency Database, social media platforms have reversed their removal decisions in 20芒聙聯35% of those cases. So we have been empowering our users and citizens. Because of the DSA, users can also see now why content on online platforms is recommended to them and they can also opt out of personalised recommendations based on profiling.
Under the DSA, we also, very recently in July, issued guidelines on the protection of minors. The scope and ambition of these guidelines are unparalleled globally, and now we are working with online platforms to make sure that they are changing their practices to also comply with these rules and following the guidelines, which very concretely state what kind of practices we are expecting from online platforms to make sure that our children have their safety, security and privacy ensured when they are using online services. Thanks to the DMA, Europeans can now choose the web browser they prefer and they also have better control over their own data.
What is true for the DSA and DMA is also true for the application of our competition rules in the digital sphere. In September, we concluded that Google breached EU antitrust rules in the AdTech industry and fined it with EUR脗聽2.95脗聽billion. Google now has 60脗聽days to present us with a viable solution. Structural remedies are likely to be the only way to cease Google's conflicts of interest in the industry on both sides of the Atlantic.脗聽We also received comprehensive commitments from Microsoft to address EU competition concerns relating to the Teams collaboration platform. This decision helps us to open crucial markets to competitors.
Some of the very large online platforms and gatekeepers have called for the DSA and DMA to be repealed or reset. Some refer to non-existent free speech concerns to avoid respecting the DSA. Some pretend to speak on behalf of European consumers, arguing that the DMA is worsening their experience. Honourable members, you surely remember the similar strong lobbying when we 芒聙聯 I was also a member of Parliament at that time 芒聙聯 were shaping and adopting these two legislations. Some of the platforms and gatekeepers do not want to accept legislation that holds them accountable to their promises or legislation that opens up their walled gardens to offer opportunities for new innovators. Let me be clear: in Europe, we believe in the force of democratic rules, not in the rule of force. It is not up to the companies to decide whether to comply with our rules, and it is not up to them or the other countries to decide how we enforce our rules. Europe decides for itself.

AI to detect diseases and discover new medicines? Perfectly allowed under European tech rules and not covered by the AI Act.
AI to make production processes more sustainable and less wasteful? Allowed under European rules and not high-risk under the AI Act.
Using AI and its outputs in research and innovation? Completely exempted from the AI Act 芒聙聯 and, actually, we encourage all of this.
Sure, some things are not allowed. AI companionship, or chatbots recommending you commit suicide? No, that's not allowed under European tech rules.
LinkedIn and Meta using all your data to train their AI systems unless you opt out (but they're making it really hard)? No, that's also not allowed under European tech rules.
Because in Europe we ensure that AI is safe, and technology actually contributes to people, society and the environment, while fostering innovation.
So, the question is: will the Commission be fooled by tech CEOs and their top lobbyist, Donald Trump? Or will the Commission stand up for European tech companies and the citizens of Europe? I think we need to be serious; if Europe wants to be taken seriously, we need more than just rules. We need to be competitive and we need to build a dynamic pro-investment environment where home-grown EU tech can thrive. Over 100 different pieces of EU legislation have been proposed in the last few years and there's still more to come. I honestly think this is madness. We need to reduce the regulatory burdens on European countries. We need to reduce the regulatory divergence. And I want to see the 'digital omnibus' bill do exactly that.
Furthermore, Europe will not develop a digital sovereign future if we cut ourselves off from the rest of the world. We need to protect our supply chains, promote digital solutions, and simply give companies the space and the freedom that they need and the support that they need to innovate. The talent and the potential is here in Europe. We need to be brave enough to let it grow and thrive.

At the same time, we are also working with the digital simplification package, with the digital omnibus, but it does not mean that we are planning to somehow roll back or lower our standards. It is that we are looking at the overlapping parts of our rules. We want to simplify things, we want to cut red tape and administrative burden from our companies to make sure that when we have, at the same time, very clear rules when it comes to the digital environment, we also have rules which are all the time encouraging new innovations and investments.
As President von der Leyen said in her State of the Union speech, it is the sovereign right of the EU and its Member States to apply our rules. We set our own standards to protect and empower our citizens and also to enable our businesses to thrive.

The Digital Services Act is one of our tools. We should not forget that, in addition, we have other legal instruments that we adopted recently 芒聙聯 in particular, the Artificial Intelligence Act; electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services; the Data Act; and the Data Governance Act. All these rules support our values and impose obligations on market players to protect European consumers.
Our rulebook applies to all companies in the same manner, no matter where they are established. All partners, including the US, must know that the EU will always defend its democracy, sovereignty, its citizens, its companies and its values.

Deswegen bin ich wirklich stolz darauf, dass es dieses Parlament in der letzten Legislaturperiode gewesen ist, das diesen Novellierungsprozess in Gang gesetzt hat, in dem wir einen Initiativbericht verfasst haben, wo wir die Kommission aufgefordert haben, die Regulierung in Gang zu bringen. Das waren in der Vergangenheit die gro脙聼en Schwachpunkte, da gab es immer wieder Streit zwischen beiden Seiten. Und da haben wir jetzt f脙录r mehr Klarheit gesorgt. Wir stellen einfach auch Dinge klar, die in vielen Mitgliedstaaten selbstverst脙陇ndlich sind, in einigen aber immer noch nicht geregelt, was n脙陇mlich zum Beispiel die Frage der Kosten脙录bernahme angeht.
Und wenn ich dann gerade in den letzten Tagen, gesteuert auch von bestimmten Verb脙陇nden, immer wieder die Bedenken h脙露re, die jetzt ins Spiel gef脙录hrt werden! Da wird gesagt: Ja, da wird sozusagen nationalen Betriebsr脙陇ten etwas weggenommen und auf die europ脙陇ische Ebene verlagert脗聽芒聙聯 das ist Quatsch. Wenn es um transnationale Ma脙聼nahmen geht, da war schon vorher auch in der Richtlinie von 2009 immer der Europ脙陇ische Betriebsrat derjenige, der zuerst informiert werden musste. Es wird auch keine neue B脙录rokratie geschaffen, sondern mehr Rechtsklarheit und mehr Rechtssicherheit ist ein Beitrag dazu, dass es besser funktioniert. Da geht es nicht um mehr B脙录rokratie.
Und von einigen wird nat脙录rlich auch die Debatte dazu genutzt, generell Mitbestimmung als Bremse darzustellen. Und gerade in meinem Heimatland Deutschland hat man sich politisch und wissenschaftlich nun wirklich intensiv mit dieser Frage besch脙陇ftigt. Deutschland als Mutterland der sozialen Marktwirtschaft, als Mutterland der Mitbestimmung in Europa kommt ganz klar zu dem Ergebnis脗聽芒聙聯 im 脙聹brigen partei脙录bergreifend, diese beiden Kommissionen, die das untersucht haben, partei脙录bergreifend: Unternehmen, die Mitbestimmung haben, funktionieren besser.
Mitbestimmung ist kein Nachteil, sondern ist ein Standortvorteil. Deswegen tragen wir mit dieser Novellierung dazu bei, dass wir insgesamt die Mitbestimmung in Europa st脙陇rken und damit auch den Standort Europa wieder attraktiver machen. Und dazu leistet diese Novellierung einen wesentlichen Beitrag.

Let me start by saying that your vote tomorrow on the revised European Works Council Directive will conclude a project marked by close collaboration, which was very much driven 芒聙聯 as I understand it, from the outset 芒聙聯 by the European Parliament. It was the Parliament's own initiative resolution of 2023 that started the process of revising the whole directive. The Commission has recognised the scope for improvement and we followed up on it.
So let me thank all of those who contributed to the successful conclusion of the negotiations. In particular, of course, the rapporteur, Mr脗聽Radtke, and also the shadow rapporteurs for the time and effort that they have put into this.
The agreement we discussed today shows that the European Union is able to act in the interests of workers and businesses alike in what we view is truly a balanced way. It takes up many of the changes that were requested by Parliament. It delivers on our shared goals: to help workers make their voice heard in big multinational corporations and to guarantee the ability of European businesses to take decisions swiftly in a demanding competitive environment.
So, honourable Members, I am convinced that a well-functioning system of employee involvement is key to manage change, in particular during companies' restructuring or when introducing new technologies.
Through constructive engagement with our Member States, your negotiators obtained tangible benefits in that regard. And please allow me, dear President, to underline some of the key achievements.
First, the new provisions will ensure that the European Works Councils are consulted in a timely and meaningful manner. Members of European Works Councils are entitled to a reasonable response before the company makes decisions on transnational matters.
Second, we will improve gender balance on European Works Councils and on special negotiating bodies. Parties must aim for at least 40脗聽% representation of each gender, and if this is not possible, workers must be informed of the reasons.
Third, we clarified when a topic qualifies as transnational, and this addresses one of the main potential sources of dispute between management on the one hand, and employee representatives on the other.
Fourth, access to justice is strengthened. Member States must ensure European Works Councils and special negotiating bodies can access justice to assert their rights.
Fifth, we provide for stronger enforcement of the rights laid down in the directive. Member States must put in place effective, dissuasive and proportionate penalties, and these should take into account the company's annual turnover or have a similarly dissuasive nature.
Dear President, these improvements represent significant progress for information and consultation on transnational matters. And at the same time, the revised directive does not create any disproportionate requirements that would actually hamper the functioning of companies or impose substantial costs.
During the negotiations, all sides were keenly aware of how important the competitiveness of European companies truly is in the current political and economic context. We have also agreed that work that European Works Councils must remain a forum for information and for consultation, without the power to either block or delay urgent company decisions. We have also ensured that companies can protect their legitimate interests in the confidentiality of sensitive information.
Regarding financial and material resources for European Works Councils, management and employee representatives will keep the necessary flexibility to agree on the right solutions. They know best the specific situation and needs in their respective companies.
Ladies and gentlemen, democracy at work remains at the heart of European Union values, even more so in this time of significant transition. In view of the Commission, we have reached a balanced and balanced compromise and I encourage you to confirm the political agreement with your vote tomorrow. But first, I very much look forward to hearing your views in today's debate.

By unifying and by 'eurofying', we create a system which is easier to navigate for businesses, while at the same time supporting employees all across the Union. The outcomes of these negotiations will make living, working and doing business in the EU more democratic.
I would like to thank the rapporteur, Dennis Radtke, for his hard work. It was not easy and we still have aspects where we differ in position, but overall I am very happy with the good compromises that we found. I think we found the right balance both for workers and for businesses in the EU.

That is not fair and that is not the Europe we believe in. That's why we changed the rules. With this new law, actually, European Works Councils will finally give workers a real voice. No more decisions taken in Paris that destroy jobs in Berlin without talking to workers first.
From now on, when a decision could affect your job, your pay, your health 芒聙聯 no matter where it's taken 芒聙聯 your voice must be heard. And when those decisions are taken, at least 40脗聽% of the room must be women, and that's always a better decision then, no?
When workers are heard, companies do better. They find solutions, they avoid layoffs and they protect people, not just profits. This is what Europe is about: making sure our citizens are heard and protected wherever they live. Making sure that borders are never an excuse to leave responsibilities somewhere else. So, I think we altogether and Dennis Radtke did a very good job, and thank you for this.

Als gewezen ondervoorzitter van de Europese ondernemingsraad van General Motors Europa weet ik perfect wat er nodig is om werknemersinspraak te verbeteren. En dat is cruciaal, want werknemersinspraak is goed voor de hele industrie. Werknemers kennen hun bedrijven beter dan wie ook en weten wat er moet worden gedaan om de zaken te verbeteren. Onderzoek wijst uit dat bedrijven die hun werknemers beter betrekken bij belangrijke beslissingen, beter functioneren dan bedrijven die dat niet doen. Werknemers hebben hun bedrijven veel meer te bieden dan enkel hun handen.
We mogen daarom deze kans niet voorbij laten gaan en moeten werknemers eindelijk geven waar ze recht op hebben. Ik kijk uit naar de stemming. Ik heb alvast een vermoeden.

Today, we have the opportunity to change that. This reform gives the European Works Councils clearer rights, stronger enforcement and binding participation in decision-making. It ensures early and meaningful consultation, not after factories and production sites are closed or jobs have disappeared, but before decisions are taken.
It also introduces tangible sanctions for companies that fail to comply. Let me be clear: this is not about opposing regulation and competitiveness. On the contrary, true competitiveness requires trust, participation and a shared sense of purpose.
When workers are genuinely involved in shaping the future of their company, they become partners in innovation and in resilience and not obstacles to change. This text is more than a revision; it is a milestone in European social dialogue. Let us make this directive not a silent formality, but a living instrument for five million European workers.

Ladies and gentlemen, I am not going to disclose a secret when I am saying that for the Commission at large, but also for me personally, making sure that we simplify things in general, but also in the domain of tax is an absolute priority. It is simply essential in our efforts to build a more competitive European Union, and with all the differences we might have, also in this House, I think this is one of the things we absolutely agree on.
What I've done is, I've sent my annual progress report on simplification, implementation and enforcement in the area of climate and taxation to the European Parliament and the Council. And what it does is it sets out all simplification measures taken between the start of my mandate and脗聽31脗聽July脗聽2025, so this year.
Ladies and gentlemen, if you were to zoom out, you would find and probably agree that what the EU needs is a tax system that, at its core, allows: first, for the money that we need to pay for all the public goods that are so close to our hearts; second, that is perceived as fair; and third, that leads to more sustainable and more economic growth. If you were to agree with that, then you would probably also agree that this requires significantly improving tax cooperation and reducing fragmentation, if you take into account the landscape as it as it currently stands.
Now the Commission is working on a recast of the Directive on Administrative Cooperation, better known to the experts as DAC4 2026. The proposal will cover several DAC directives. The recast proposal will seek to simplify processes while enhancing legal certainty for businesses. That is, of course, of utmost importance as well. We are not doing this in isolation. It is grounded in truly extensive consultations and will also have an impact assessment, as it should.
Also, the Commission is undertaking an evaluation of the Anti-Tax Avoidance Directive and mapping the operations of the Corporate Tax Directive, and we are considering presenting a proposal on simplifying the tax acquis in 2026. That will, amongst other things, clarify how Pillar Two interacts with existing legislation.
Another contribution to simplification is VAT in a digital age, known to all of you here as ViDA, where the Commission and Member States are now moving towards its implementation, and on which this House's support is absolutely crucial. The ViDA package is expected to generate EUR脗聽51脗聽billion in savings for businesses. Let me pause and repeat: EUR脗聽51脗聽billion. It is an absolute game changer if you look at the stuff we are typically talking about. If it is correctly implemented 芒聙聯 and of course, there we have a huge responsibility 芒聙聯 if it is correctly implemented across our internal market.
The Commission took a significant step forward by rolling out the ViDA implementation strategy in September脗聽2025. I am stepping up these efforts by convening an implementation dialogue with business leaders that will take place on 28脗聽October. Of course, I would be happy to share our thinking on that as well.
Now, the Commission will also look at challenges, of course, beyond ViDA. We will ensure that Member States receive the top analytical capabilities for cross芒聙聭border VAT data, because there's simply more to be had in this specific domain. We'll also create an integrated fund against fraud, and that should involve Eurofisc, OLAF and, of course, EPPO.
Honourable Members, finally, your report calls for greater coordination of tax incentives across our Member States. And it is good to, in this realm, articulate that the Commission has only very recently 芒聙聯 last week 芒聙聯 put forward a recommendation on tax incentives to support investments in clean technologies and industrial decarbonisation.
Moreover, we have adopted a recommendation laying down a blueprint for the savings and investments accounts, which also includes tax elements. Just to be correct, that specific last part was the thing we did last week. It sets out a comprehensive framework to help Member States design cost芒聙聭effective tax measures that boost investment in these hugely important sectors.
It is also critical that tax legislation be consistently interpreted across Member States to actually reduce the burden on all stakeholders involved. I cannot stress it enough 芒聙聯 we need that for our competitiveness, and that is why we will come up with common guidance, which will then help stakeholders to effectively and uniformly interpret and apply EU directives in the field of taxation. It might sound trivial, but it is hugely complex and very, very important.
So that is where we stand. I am very much looking forward to the debate. Once again, many thanks for all the great work that has gone into this. The good news is it will stay with us for years to come.

Firstly, it deters cross芒聙聭border investments, so not all EU firms can tap the same pool of finance. Secondly, it imposes heavy compliance costs on those who clear the hurdle and manage to finally operate across borders.脗聽The outcome is both predictable and dismal. Too many EU companies cannot compete effectively and they fall further behind their US peers.
The solution, I think, is not a fully harmonised tax system. Tax and institutional competition should remain a source of dynamism, provided it happens on a foundation that is both fair and practical. The real answer, in my opinion, lies in a simplified and more efficient fiscal framework that helps companies finance themselves and meet their obligations without devoting valuable resources to compliance costs.
Simplification is necessary, but not sufficient. For companies to remain innovative, we must provide the right incentives. Rather than relaxing the State aid framework or considering to tax companies operating in the single market 芒聙聯 as proposed in the current core proposal for own resources 芒聙聯 I think we should rather prioritise fairer solutions, such as transferable tax credits for innovative investments recognised across borders.
This approach would truly level the playing field, crowd in private capital and foster innovation while preserving national tax regimes. The path is clear: fewer barriers, simpler rules, more innovation and stronger competitiveness.

Tutti bellissimi propositi, colleghi, che continuiamo a leggere, che continuiamo a sentire ma che non vediamo tradotti ancora in proposte di legge nella misura in cui ne avremmo bisogno in questo particolare momento storico.
L'Unione europea dovrebbe smettere immediatamente di regolamentare e dovrebbe iniziare seriamente a semplificare ma anche eliminare tutte quelle norme che ci hanno portato a essere in questa situazione e hanno creato quell'enorme gap in termini di competitivit脙聽, innovazione, investimenti nei confronti delle economie pi脙鹿 avanzate.

We keep saying that to make Europe more competitive, we need to re-energize our European companies in the single market. But the companies I speak to in my constituency have boundless energy. However, they need to spend a lot of it on navigating complex regulation or overcoming regulatory hurdles.
We also say that we need to motivate our companies to invest and grow, but the companies I speak to are not lacking in motivation. The issue is that they need to plough through a lot of their money in compliance costs. In fact, a European Commission study found that the annual tax compliance bill for companies in the EU and the UK stands at around 200 billion, and a disproportionate amount of this falls on to our small and medium-sized enterprises.
I fully support this report on simplifying tax rules and addressing tax fragmentation. In this report, we take a very practical and feasible approach to simplifying the framework. Too often, this House immediately jumps to harmonisation as a solution to problems. But such an approach gets stuck in the weeds of politics in Council and Parliament, and there is no benefit to European companies.
Instead, this report focuses on practical changes that can be made in the short term to ease the day to day processes of doing business. We need to streamline the framework and ensure certainty and predictability so our businesses can be confident in making decisions in the future. We need to improve the efficiency of tax administration and digitise where possible. And there should be a level of continuity amongst the interfaces of the different tax authorities, so when a company in Ireland engages with the authority in France, for example, there is a familiarity.
So I want to commend the report and again thank the order and hope that we act upon it.

Large companies, multinationals, can even benefit from fragmentation. But the EU's competitiveness is not improved by confusing bureaucracy.
I also would like to thank the rapporteur for his very good work, which was rewarding to participate in. The whole takes into account all the different interests as we have heard today.
Simplification and coordination between Member States in tackling tax evasion, fraud and avoidance is important to facilitate cross-border economic activity.

The summit will be historical: for the first time in 30脗聽years, the world will come together for a global conference dedicated to social development. The last time was in Copenhagen in 1995. Back then, leaders set out a vision to fight poverty, create decent jobs and strengthen social justice. Three decades later, it's time to look at how far we have come and where we are falling short.
We have made some progress, but in recent years, crises have hit the world hard 芒聙聯 a pandemic, wars, economic shocks, social and environmental crisis. These setbacks have pushed millions back into poverty, widened inequalities and reversed decades of progress. Your resolution puts the spotlight not only on global challenges, but also on what we must do within the European Union. The credibility of our voice on the world stage depends on the strength of our actions at home.
Our guiding compass is the European Pillar of Social Rights and its action plan. It is our way of making sure that social fairness is behind every policy we shape, and help us to achieve our sustainable development goals. The resolution refers to the three headline targets on employment, skills and poverty reduction. We concur with the assessment that while we have almost achieved the employment objective, there is still much to do to lift the share of people in training and to reduce poverty.
That is why the European Commission has put forward the 'union of skills' strategy. Just to mention one example, with the pact for skills, we are committed to training more than 25脗聽million people by 2030. On poverty elimination, we will propose an EU anti芒聙聭poverty strategy that is aimed at helping people access the essential protection and services that they need and addresses the root causes of poverty.
We will also strengthen the European Child Guarantee, supporting Member States in combating child poverty and tackling social exclusion through education, healthcare and other essential services. The Commission will propose a quality jobs roadmap aimed at supporting good working conditions and fair transition. As President脗聽von der Leyen announced during the State of the Union, this will pave the way to a quality jobs act as the脗聽centrepiece of the roadmap.
None of this is possible without strong social dialogue. We know that Europe works best when workers and employers shape the future together, and that is why, together with our social partners, we have agreed a new pact for European social dialogue. At global level, we aim for meaningful progress in these areas through effective multilateralism, inclusive international cooperation, and shared responsibility with our partners, taking into account national realities and regional contexts, particularly in developing countries and fragile contexts.
Honourable Members, the challenges ahead are immense, but so is the responsibility that we share. Together, we can make sure that Europe continues to lead by example.
