Our efforts paved the way to Iran agreeing to limit its nuclear programme; the agreement was the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action. Since then, it is clear that Iran has departed from its commitments, as evidenced by more than 60 IAEA reports over six years. The Iranian nuclear programme lacks any credible civilian justification and is unprecedented for a state without a nuclear weapons programme.
Also, for more than five years, the International Atomic Energy Agency could not carry out important verification and monitoring activities. The situation has led France, Germany and the UK to notify the United Nations Security Council of Iran's non芒聙聭compliance under the脗聽Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action. With the notification, they invoke the process known as the snapback mechanism. Intensive diplomatic efforts with Iran during a 30-day timeframe foreseen in the snapback process took place. Unfortunately, the result of these efforts did not allow for a diplomatic solution as Iran did not engage constructively.
As a consequence, the UN Security Council has reimposed all nuclear芒聙聭related UN sanctions and restrictions against Iran. The EU has already reimposed all UN and also relevant EU sanctions that we previously lifted. We call on the international community to implement these sanctions.
Let me be clear, the snapback of sanctions and nuclear restrictions does not mean the end of diplomacy with Iran. In fact, a sustainable solution to the Iranian nuclear issue can only be achieved through negotiations and diplomacy. But the prerequisite is that Iran returns to the negotiation table in a meaningful way. The EU, together with partners in the region, will continue to engage in this key issue for regional and international security.

The EU has been supportive of the JCPOA and has fully implemented it, despite unprecedented challenges, including the departure of the US from the agreement in 2018, the reimposition of the extension of US sanctions, as well as the subsequent actions taken by Iran in contradiction to its JCPOA commitments.
The reinstated UN sanctions covers three main categories. First, the nuclear program itself, which would again be under a binding obligation to suspend all enrichment芒聙聭related and reprocessing activities. Second, Iran's ballistic missile and military capabilities. And third, financial and economic restrictions.脗聽We call on the international community to implement these sanctions.
The EU is implementing the return of UN sanctions along with EU autonomous restrictive measures. This has been done under Iran's weapons of mass destruction regime. Our EU measures include, first, travel bans for individuals, asset freezes for individuals and entities, and in line with this, the prohibition on making funds or economic resources available to those listed.
Second, economic and financial sanctions covering the trade, financial and transport sectors. Allow me to mention that the EU never lifted the arms embargo, nor the listing of the Iranian Revolutionary Guards in line with the mechanism of the JCPOA.脗聽The European Commission will follow closely the implementation of the reimposed sanctions.
EU sanctions are targeted and designed to avoid unintended negative consequences to the Iranian population. EU sanctions are not meant to stand in the way nor impede the supply of humanitarian aid, including medical assistance. EU measures comply with all obligations under international law, in particular international human rights law, international refugee law and international humanitarian law.
With the reimposition of all previously lifted UN and EU sanctions, we are now in a post芒聙聭JCPOA phase. As the HR/VP has made clear, this post芒聙聭JCPOA phase will not mark the end of diplomacy with Iran on the nuclear issue. We continue to believe that a sustainable solution to the Iranian nuclear issue, which remains a key challenge to the region and international security, can only be achieved through dialogue and diplomacy.

The introduction of sanctions is therefore both welcome and long overdue. The sanctions should be meaningful, fully enforced and closely monitored.
But let's not forget that this is not the issue. The real issue is the Iranian regime, which is a challenge that requires an entirely new approach of our policy towards Teheran.
With the Iranian regime in place, there will be no peace in the region. The Ayatollahs took good care to destroy the Abraham Accords through their proxies: Hamas, Houthis and Hezbollah. And now, as the new opportunity for peace emerges in the region, Tehran will do whatever they can to disrupt the peace process.
With the Iranian regime in place Russia will continue to have a useful ally in its aggression against Ukraine. With the Iranian regime in place, brutal repression with no legal or moral limits will continue to fall on anyone who dares to challenge the tyranny. Over 1000 people executed in the first nine months of 2025.
So at this point, let me ask, why do Iranian ambassadors live so comfortably in European capitals? And why is the listing of the Revolutionary Guard as a terrorist organisation still not included in the sanctions adopted, despite the consistent and strong request from this Parliament?
Dear colleagues, many Iranians inside and outside Iran risked their lives. They deserve to be supported, to be protected and to share their commitment for a free and democratic Iran.

Iran is weaker than ever, and yet can still count on a solid alliance with Russia, and particularly also with China, which are the main buyers of Iran's oil.
The West at this point has two options: taking advantage of this crisis, but risking a major escalation, or making Iran part of a larger, what we once called a 'comprehensive peace process', a process that could involve and stabilise all the major players of the region, Israel, Arab nations and Iran and global players as US, Europe and China.
It is the type of process that I like to feel that a little bit catch Ms Kallas in her recent statement.

Strengthening respect for the rule of law and promoting human rights remains a long-standing key priority for the European Union. We always emphasise that states must take active steps to protect human rights.
Unfortunately, impunity for human rights violations and abuses and corruption remain far too widespread. In Equatorial Guinea, we are deeply concerned about the prison conditions and ill treatment of and violence against detainees, and the use of torture, especially in prisons, continues to be reported and widespread. Prisons continue to be overcrowded and imprisonment conditions remain harsh.

The authorities should ensure access to detainees to legal assistance and to contact with their families.
Exchange and progress on human rights, including prison conditions and arbitrary detentions are necessary for the incentivisation of our relations with the country. The EU encourages the authorities of Equatorial Guinea to make concrete progress on the recommendations of the recent Universal Periodic Review, and expresses its readiness to support these efforts.

What we are witnessing is the erosion of law and humanity itself. Haiti's suffering is a humanitarian catastrophe and a moral test for the whole international community. Europe cannot remain a spectator. We must act with determination, grounded in our values and guided by responsibility.
Firstly, we must restore justice and accountability. Gangs act because impunity reigns. The European Union should help rebuild Haiti's judicial capacity, training judges, investigators and prosecutors. And we have to support the investigation of these massacres and corruption. Without justice, there will be no stability.
Secondly, we must intensify humanitarian aid. The UN Humanitarian Response Plan remains gravely underfunded. Europe should step up: food assistance, healthcare and protection of those most in need; ensuring transparency, monitoring and secure access for humanitarian workers.
Thirdly, we must help re-establish security and the rule of law. The UN芒聙聭mandated Gang Suppression Force, approved by the Security Council, must be staffed properly, equipped and financed. The European Union should support this effort politically and technically.
And so, coming to a conclusion, I think this massacre in Cabaret must serve as a turning point. By adopting this resolution, our Parliament sends a united and clear message: Europe stands with the people of Haiti, works against terror and works for security, justice and restoration of human dignity.

The tragic toll of more than 3脗聽000 lives lost since the beginning of the year underlines the urgent need for swift and decisive action. This tragic reality was most recently illustrated by the attack in the commune of Cabaret in September, which claimed at least 40脗聽lives 芒聙聯 a horrible act that the EU delegation in Haiti and locally represented Member States were swift to condemn.
Equally devastating is the use of sexual violence by these gangs as a way to control and intimidate people. This makes the situation even worse, spreading fear and trauma among those affected.
Despite these challenges, there is a sign of hope.
In this context, we welcome the recent UN Security Council resolution reinforcing the Multinational Security Support Mission, whose numbers will be increased and whose financing will be facilitated. These resources will be strengthened through an assessed UN contribution, and a new UN logistics office will be established to ensure coordination and operational support. This new, stronger force will play a key role in fighting these armed gangs, hopefully bringing back peace and keeping people safe in Haiti.
We support the international effort and, with the intermediary of the Organization of American States, are financing the construction of forward operating bases that shall aid the security missions with a EUR脗聽10脗聽million grant.
We have always supported Haiti and continue to do so, currently with more than EUR脗聽300脗聽million in ongoing projects for development and humanitarian aid. For 2025 alone, EUR脗聽39.5脗聽million has been set aside specifically for humanitarian needs in Haiti, including EUR脗聽33脗聽million through our humanitarian partners on the ground and EUR脗聽6.4脗聽million to ensure the functioning of the UNHAS helicopter service.
Humanitarian aid is essential, but not sufficient alone. A nexus approach, addressing all the components of this complex crisis 芒聙聯 political, security, humanitarian and development, including the regional dimension with Dominican Republic 芒聙聯 remains the only path forward for a durable solution. Massive investments in social protection are needed and access to food and basic services, health and water sanitation must be urgently re-established.
The crisis in Haiti does not just affect the country itself. It also has a significant effect across the entire Caribbean. Illegal arms and drug trafficking are fuelling gang activities, impacting not only the Caribbean, but also reaching Europe. This calls for a shared responsibility and it is something that we cannot ignore.

Gui's case is not an isolated domestic matter. It is emblematic of a worrying practice of transnational repressions, disappearance of people for expressing freedom of thought and freedom of belief, and forced confessions, which are orchestrated during sham trials. And this is not only about the case of Gui Minhai 芒聙聯 it's Ilham Tohti, Jimmy Lai, Panchen Lama, but also Ding Jiaxi, thousands of other political prisoners in China, millions of Uighurs who are held in concentration camps and labour camps, and many more suffer under the brutal regime of Beijing.
I grew up in the then communist regime in Czechoslovakia. These regimes do not play according to international rules. They are either exploiting them or violating them. We must finally understand this and act accordingly. Again and again, I call for a principled approach to the Chinese communist regime, for an approach in which human rights and fundamental freedoms are the central pillar of our relations, because respect for fundamental freedoms is non-negotiable.

Gui Minhai was sentenced to two years and released in October 2017. He was re-arrested in 2018 and, two years later, in February 2020, sentenced to ten years' imprisonment, accused of providing intelligence for foreign powers.
Until today, the exact whereabouts of Gui Minhai are not known. His family has been unable to contact him in prison. There is no reliable information concerning his health condition. Moreover, in a clear contravention of the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations, Gui Minhai's rights to consular assistance were not respected. The Chinese authorities have denied all requests for consular access, claiming that he renounced his Swedish citizenship, whereas Sweden maintains that he is still a Swedish citizen, and thus also an EU citizen.
Gui Minhai is a strong proponent of freedom of speech and media freedom. He has authored numerous books expressing his views on Chinese politics, and founded a publishing house in Hong Kong.
We now know that his work has come at a high cost for him and his family, as Evin Incir and also Jonas Sj脙露stedt talked about. His detention is symbolic of the Chinese Government's systematic suppression of fundamental freedoms and its concerning crackdown on human rights defenders, lawyers, journalists and academics.
We have consistently voiced our grave concern about the human rights situation in China and resolutely called for the unconditional release of all those detained for peacefully exercising their fundamental freedoms and human rights. We reiterate our calls on China to release Gui Minhai, as repeatedly put forward at the EU-China Human Rights Dialogue, in high-level meetings with Chinese interlocutors, as well as in public statements, including at the UN Human Rights Council. We further urge China to respect Gui Minhai's right to consular assistance and ensure contact with his family.
Allow me to assure Parliament that we will continue to express its concerns about the serious human rights situation in China, and to urge China to abide by its obligations under international and national law.
